coredata.dc:title: Facile and size-controllable fabrication of copper nanoparticles on nitrogen-doped carbon dots using an N,N-dimethylformamide-based reduction approach
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 The Author(s). Published by Elsevier B.V.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Author(s). Published by Elsevier B.V.
coredata.dc:creator: Kaittidanusorn, Nattaporn
Nagata, Tatsuki
Juthathan, Methasit
Tabaru, Kazuki
Siengdung, Nattapon
Pienpinijtham, Prompong
Tanaka, Tatsuya
Suzuki, Takeyuki
Tuntulani, Thawatchai
Leeladee, Pannee
Obora, Yasushi
coredata.dc:description: Nitrogen-doped carbon dots (NCDs) have been recognized as a promising supporting material for stabilizing metal nanoparticles. In this study, copper nanoparticles (CuNPs) decorated onto NCDs were easily prepared under mild conditions. Two types of Cu fabricated NCDs were prepared by using different Cu precursors – CuCl2 and N,N-dimethylformamide (DMF)-protected CuNPs, where DMF was employed as a multifunctional reagent serving as a reducing agent, stabilizer, and solvent for the synthesis of the CuNPs. The resulting NCDs with Cu species were characterized using ICP-AES, FT-IR, XPS, XRD, and STEM. We found that the Cu precursor greatly influence the fabrication of Cu species on NCDs, with the Cu content of the CuNPs/NCDs being almost twice that of CuCl2/NCDs. Furthermore, when DMF-protected CuNPs were used, nano-sized Cu particles ranging from 4 to 11 nm were immobilized on NCDs surface, whereas single-atom Cu were observed when using CuCl2. This indicates that DMF-protected CuNPs are a suitable source of Cu for the preparation of nano-sized Cu on NCDs.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Nitrogen-doped carbon dots
Copper nanoparticles
N,N-dimethylformamide
Size-controllable fabrication

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Quantitative analysis of the Cu content on NCDs, CuCl2/NCDs and CuNPs/NCDs using ICP-AES. (b) FT-IR spectra of NCDs, CuCl2/NCDs and CuNPs/NCDs.
XPS spectra of NCDs: High-resolution spectra of (a) C 1s (b) O 1s (c) N 1s.
STEM images of (a) CuCl2/NCDs and (b) CuNPs/NCDs (scale bar = 50 nm).
DMF-based reduction approach for the preparation of (1) CuNPs/NCDs and (2) CuCl2/NCDs.

head.ce:title: Facile and size-controllable fabrication of copper nanoparticles on nitrogen-doped carbon dots using an N,N-dimethylformamide-based reduction approach

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Faculty of Science, Chulalongkorn University, Bangkok 10330, Thailand
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, Faculty of Science, Chulalongkorn University, Bangkok 10330, Thailand

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Chemistry and Materials Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry and Materials Engineering, Faculty of Chemistry, Materials and Bioengineering, Kansai University, Suita, Osaka 564-8680, Japan

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Comprehensive Analysis Center, SANKEN, Osaka University, 8-1 Mihogaoka, Ibaraki, Osaka 567-0057, Japan

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: National Nanotechnology Center of Advanced Structural and Functional Materials, Faculty of Science, Chulalongkorn University, Pathumwan, Bangkok 10330, Thailand

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: National Nanotechnology Center of Advanced Structural and Functional Materials

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: National Nanotechnology Center of Advanced Structural and Functional Materials, Faculty of Science, Chulalongkorn University, Pathumwan, Bangkok 10330, Thailand

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: N,N-dimethylformamide (DMF) was discovered to act as a function-integrated reagent in preparing copper nanoparticles — reductant, stabilizer, and solvent. Herein, Cu-decorated onto nitrogen-doped carbon dots (NCDs) were successfully prepared using different copper sources (i.e., DMF-protected CuNPs and CuCl2). The former case revealed nano-sized copper particles whereas the latter exhibited atomically dispersed copper onto NCDs.

Nitrogen-doped carbon dots (NCDs) have been recognized as a promising supporting material for stabilizing metal nanoparticles. In this study, copper nanoparticles (CuNPs) decorated onto NCDs were easily prepared under mild conditions. Two types of Cu fabricated NCDs were prepared by using different Cu precursors – CuCl2 and N,N-dimethylformamide (DMF)-protected CuNPs, where DMF was employed as a multifunctional reagent serving as a reducing agent, stabilizer, and solvent for the synthesis of the CuNPs. The resulting NCDs with Cu species were characterized using ICP-AES, FT-IR, XPS, XRD, and STEM. We found that the Cu precursor greatly influence the fabrication of Cu species on NCDs, with the Cu content of the CuNPs/NCDs being almost twice that of CuCl2/NCDs. Furthermore, when DMF-protected CuNPs were used, nano-sized Cu particles ranging from 4 to 11 nm were immobilized on NCDs surface, whereas single-atom Cu were observed when using CuCl2. This indicates that DMF-protected CuNPs are a suitable source of Cu for the preparation of nano-sized Cu on NCDs.

ce:section.ce:para: Metal nanoparticles (MNPs) have been used in numerous applications, including energy storage and conversion [1,2], biosensors [3–5], and catalysis [6–8]. In particular, copper nanoparticles (CuNPs) are popular in both research and practical applications because of their abundance, inexpensiveness and outstanding properties [1,9,10]. In fact, rapid advances have been made towards developing synthetic methodologies to enable the preparation of CuNPs with tunable sizes, shapes, and structures [4,5]. Samim and coworkers demonstrated that CuNPs with sizes of 5–8 nm exhibit a higher catalytic activity than copper powder in Ullmann condensation [6]. Additionally, the Mondal’s group reported graphene-supported CuNPs encapsulated with silica for size-dependent oxidative coupling of aromatic amines – high and selective conversion of benzylamine was observed for small CuNPs with the average size of 7.8 ± 0.8 nm, whereas larger one, i.e., 60 ± 0.5 nm, resulted in a lower catalytic activity [7]. However, the use of CuNPs is restricted by their inherent instability, as they can easily aggregate to form large agglomerates and rapidly be deactivated, especially under harsh conditions [3]. To address this concern, several research groups have recently focused on increasing the stability of CuNPs using supporting materials such as functionalized polymers [8], inorganic solids [11], and carbonaceous materials [12,13].

Carbon dots (CDs) are nanocarbon-based materials which have received much attention in the fields of energy and catalysis because of their favorable characteristics, including high aqueous dispersibility, chemical inertness, low toxicity, large specific surface area and facile modification [14]. These unique properties make CDs a great candidate for supporting materials. Liu et al. reported that MNPs on CDs exhibited good catalytic performance for photocatalysis under visible light [15]. Nonetheless, CuNPs on carbon materials are easily liberated from the surfaces of carbon materials during a catalytic reaction at high temperature. Doping nitrogen onto carbon materials – especially the nitrogen at the edges of vacant sites – causes the strong coordination between Cu ion and nitrogen, potentially stabilizing and preventing severe agglomeration of Cu ions [16,17]. It should be noted that not only nitrogen- but also oxygen-containing functional groups can act as the coordination sites to Cu ions. In contrast, without nitrogen in graphene sheets resulted in the agglomeration of copper oxide nanocluster with the size of ca. 20 nm [16]. In addition, Bulushev and coworkers showed that CuNPs exhibit a strong interaction with N-doped carbon materials, resulting in more stable CuNPs in smaller clusters (<5 nm) than undoped carbon materials [18].

MNPs decorated onto carbon materials can be prepared via electrodeposition or chemical reduction approach [3,19,20]. In general, the chemical reduction approach requires strong reductants (e.g., NaBH4, hydrazine) and surface stabilizers or capping agents to prevent the undesired agglomeration [9,21,22]. However, their hazardous and environmentally unfriendly characteristics of NaBH4 and hydrazine has limited their practicality in large-scale application. Moreover, the presence of stabilizers or capping agents such as thiolate and phosphine surfactants, as well as polymers, may prevent access to catalytically active surface sites [23]. Seeking out less toxic and surfactant-free synthesis approach results in a solution syntheses of N,N-dimethylformamide (DMF)-protected metal nanoparticles, which is a potential method for the preparation of MNPs under mild conditions. In this method, DMF has three important roles (i.e., solvent, reductant and stabilizer) [24,25]. Therefore, DMF-based reduction is amenable to synthesize the thermally stable MNPs without an additional stabilizer or capping agent. In the recent work, we reported a successful fabrication of CuNPs on reduced graphene oxide (CuNPs/rGO) via one-pot DMF reduction approach with an addition of nominal water [26]. Interestingly. copper precursor types (CuCl2 and CuNPs) were found to have a huge influence on the Cu deposition onto the supporting materials, rGO.

Herein, a facile preparation of nano-sized Cu supported on the nitrogen-doped carbon dots (NCDs) using a DMF-based reduction approach with the different of Cu sources, i.e., DMF-protected CuNPs (to prepare CuNPs/NCDs) and CuCl2 (to prepare CuCl2/NCDs) was investigated. Our work proposed the facile and less toxic approach for synthesizing CuNPs and single-atom Cu decorated onto NCDs. In addition, we showed that the types of Cu precursor have a huge impact on the Cu species existing on NCDs, which will pave the way to explore interesting aspects and will be, in hope, amenable to explore the distinct properties of future application.

L-tryptophan (>98.5 % purity) was purchased from Tokyo Chemical Industry. Hydrochloric acid (HCl, 37 % purity) was obtained from Merck. Copper(II) chloride dihydrate (CuCl2·2H2O, 98 % purity) was purchased from Carlo Erba. N,N-Dimethylformamide (HCON(CH3)2, 99.8 % purity for HPLC) and methanol (CH3OH, 99.9 % purity) were obtained from FUJIFILM Wako Pure Chemical Corporation and RCI Labscan. Potassium bromide (KBr, ≥99.0 % purity) was purchased from Sigma-Aldrich. All chemicals were used as received without prior purification. The elemental composition of the materials was observed using a scanning transmission electron microscopy (STEM) with an energy dispersive X-ray spectrometer (JEOL, JEM-ARM 200F). Fourier-transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra were recorded using a Shimadzu IR Affinity-1. Elemental analyses were carried out using THERMO Flash 2000 CHNS/O Analyzers. Inductively coupled plasma-atomic emission spectroscopy (ICP-AES) data were obtained using a Shimadzu ICPS-8100 spectrometer. X-ray photoelectron spectra (XPS) were recorded using a ULVAC-PHI PHI5000 VersaProbe instrument with Al Kα radiation. Dynamic light scattering (DLS) and zeta potentail measurements were taken using a ZetaSizer (Malvern Panalytical, NanoZS 90).

NCDs were synthesized via a hydrothermal method. L-tryptophan (6.25 mmol) was dissolved in 1 M HCl (25 mL). The solution was then transferred into a Teflon-lined hydrothermal reactor and heated in an oven at 200 °C for 12 h. After cooling to room temperature, the obtained dark brown mixture was centrifuged at 15,000 rpm for 30 min to separate out the large particles, and the suspension was dialyzed using a dialysis membrane (molecular weight cut off = 1 kDa) in deionized water for 48 h. The NCD colloid was dried using a freeze-drying method and obtained in the form of black solid powder.

DMF (50 mL) was pre-heated at 140 °C for 5 min prior to the addition of CuCl2 (0.1 M, 0.50 mL). Then, the mixture was vigorously stirred at 140 °C and 1500 rpm for 24 h. The solution of CuCl2 turned yellow in color, which is indicative of CuNP formation.

NCDs (10 mg) were dispersed in 6.25 mL of DMF in a three-neck round-bottom flask and sonicated for 30 min. Then, 1 mM of CuNPs (6.25 mL) was added to the suspension, which was sonicated for a further 30 min. The mixture was stirred vigorously at 1500 rpm at 140 °C for 24 h. After cooling, the solvent was removed by evaporation and the product was washed with MeOH (5 mL) three times. It was then centrifuged at 6000 rpm for 20 min. The CuNPs/NCDs nanocomposite was obtained as a black solid after drying in vacuo.

NCDs (10 mg) were dispersed in 6.25 mL of DMF in a three-neck round-bottom flask and sonicated for 30 min. The suspension was pre-heated at 140 °C for 5 min, then 50 μL of 0.1 M CuCl2 was added. The mixture was stirred vigorously at 1500 rpm at 140 °C for 24 h. After cooling, the solvent was removed by evaporation and the product was washed with MeOH (5 mL) three times. It was then centrifuged at 6000 rpm for 20 min. The CuCl2/NCDs nanocomposite was obtained as a black solid after drying in vacuo.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of NCDs, CuCl2/NCDs and CuNPs/NCDs
ce:section.ce:para: For the characterization, zeta (ζ)-potential measurement was performed to investigate the colloidal stability of pristine NCDs (Fig. S13 and Table S7). The result showed that the particle size and zeta potential of NCDs are 715.4 nm and −7.629 mV, respectively. In charged colloidal system, there is a consensus that ζ-potential between −30 mV and +30 mV are considered electrostatically unstable and prone to aggregation or flocculation owing to the dominant attractive Van der Waals interaction [27,28]. Therefore, NCDs, in this work, are colloidal unstable and prone to aggregation. This result was consistent with the observed aggregation of NCDs under Scanning transmission electron microscopy (STEM) images (Fig. S11).

Surface functional groups and compositions of NCDs, CuCl2/NCDs and CuNPs/NCDs were characterized using Fourier-transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR) and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). All NCDs exhibit a strong and broad adsorption peak at 3500–3100 cm−1 which belongs to stretching vibrations of O-H and N-H bonds, and two weak peaks at ca. 2900–2800 cm−1 which are assignable to stretching vibrations of (sp2)C-H and (sp3)C-H [29,30]. The adsorption peak 1590 cm−1 is indicative of CC stretching of the aromatic sp2 carbon. In addition, the stretching vibration of N-CO, NC, heterocyclic C-N-C, N-(C)3 bonds appears at 1640, 1435, 1333 and 1230 cm−1 [31,32]. At the fingerprint region, there is a sharp peak at 745 cm−1 which is attributed to the bending vibration of N-H [31]. Therefore, FT-IR spectra suggested the existence of –OH, –NH2, –CONH2 groups on the surface of NCDs. In addition, the elemental analysis of pristine NCDs confirmed that NCDs comprise of C, H and N with the elemental composition with 9.28 %wt N dopant (Table S5).

XPS was used to reveal the elemental composition of the as-prepared NCDs, CuCl2/NCDs, and CuNPs/NCDs. The survey XPS spectrum of the pristine NCDs shows distinct signals of C, N, and O on the surface (Fig. S1). The XPS survey spectra of the CuCl2/NCDs and CuNPs/NCDs are similar to that of the NCDs, indicating that the major constituents of these materials are C, N, and O (Fig. S2).

C 1s, O 1s, and N 1s XPS profiles of bare NCDs, CuCl2/NCDs, and CuNPs/NCDs were consistent with the previous related literature [33,34], albeit no significant shifts were observed upon functionalization of Cu (Figs. S2-S4). Peak positions and assignments of XPS spectra for NCDs, CuCl2/NCDs, and CuNPs/NCDs were summarized in Tables S2-S4 . The high-resolution spectrum of C 1s for bare NCDs were resolved in five peaks related to C(sp3) at 283.3 eV, C(sp2) at 283.8 eV, C(C-N) at 284.5 eV, and C(O-C = O) at 286.9 eV (Fig. 2 (a)) [35–38]. The deconvoluted O 1s spectrum had four peaks, which were attributed to oxygen atoms in amide carbonyl at 530.3 eV, carbonyl at 531.1 eV, hydroxyl at 532.1 eV, and ester at 533.2 eV (Fig. 2 (b)) [38,39]. Three major types of nitrogen dopants contributed to pyridinic N, pyrrolic N, and basal N were confirmed by the N 1s spectrum, and these dopants were located at 398.3 eV, 399.3 eV, and 400.5 eV, respectively (Fig. 2 (c)) [38,40].

The presence of Cu on the CuNPs/NCDs and CuCl2/NCDs was confirmed using the split of the Cu 2p1/2 and Cu 2p3/2 peaks in the Cu 2p region (Fig. S5). The Cu 2p spectrum of the CuNPs/NCDs showed binding energies of 932.2 and 952.2 eV, which are corresponding to the Cu 2p1/2 and Cu 2p3/2 peaks. Likewise, for the as-prepared CuCl2/NCDs, the peaks derived from the Cu 2p1/2 and Cu 2p3/2 were observed at 932.7 and 952.6 eV, respectively. There were no large satellite peaks from 938 to 946 eV, indicating the absence of Cu(II) species in the materials [41]. Here, it was difficult to assign Cu(0) or Cu(I) in the Cu 2p3/2 peaks because their biding energy values are located in almost the same region [42]. The Wagner plot, refers to a plot of the Auger kinetic energy on the vertical axis and the electron binding energy on the horizontal axis oriented in a reversed direction, is a powerful characterization tool to assess chemical properties such as local valences states of the copper species [43–45]. The Wagner plot for the CuNPs/NCDs, CuCl2/NCDs and related copper species was illustrated in Fig. S6 , and the binding and kinetic energy was shown in Table S6. In both cases, Cu(I) were domain rather than Cu(0) on the NCDs.

Scanning transmission electron microscopy (STEM) showed the presence of nano-sized Cu on the surface of the NCDs when using the DMF-protected CuNPs approach (Fig. 3 (b)). The distribution of Cu was observed on the surface of NCDs by using energy-dispersive spectroscopy (EDS) coupled with the elemental mapping; in addition, the spot on the NCDs was confirmed to be nano-sized Cu by EDS analysis (Fig. S8-S9). The particle size distribution of the CuNPs on the NCDs was observed to be in the range 4–11 nm, with an average diameter of ∼ 7.7 nm (Fig. S10).

In previous works, DMF-stabilized CuNPs with an average size of 2–7 nm was prepared via the DMF-reduction method. This indicates that functionalization of DMF-stablized CuNPs on NCDs does not significantly alter the particle size of the CuNPs. However, the CuNPs on the NCDs were larger (4–11 nm) than the DMF-stabilized CuNPs and CuNPs/rGO (∼3–5 nm) reported in recent works [24–26]. This is likely to a further reduction of DMF during the functionalization of the CuNPs. In contrast, no nano-sized Cu particles were observed for the CuCl2/NCDs (Fig. 3 (a)). However, EDS coupled with elemental mapping revealed that elemental Cu was evenly distributed on the NCDs surface of CuCl2/NCDs (Fig. S7). The X-ray diffraction (XRD) profile of the CuCl2/NCDs was not diagnostic, whereas diffraction patterns were observed for the CuNPs/NCDs (Fig. S12). This can be attributed to the presence of a small crystallite size, resulting in the formation of undetectable crystal structures. These results, together with the ICP-AES analysis, confirms the presence of Cu in a near single-atom state (<0.1 nm) in the CuCl2/NCDs. STEM analysis of pristine NCDs was carried out as a control and showed only aggregated hundred-nanometer scale carbon dots (Fig. S11). With these data in hand, we proposed the schematic illustration to represent the structure and preparation of CuNPs/NCDs and CuCl2/NCDs via DMF-based reduction as shown in Scheme 1.

To compare whether NCDs or rGO has the better effect of stabilization, the particle size of copper dispersed on the supports would be discussed. It should be noted that the effect of stabilization was compared in the case of CuCl2 precursor, since, in this scenario, the copper ion is likely to interact with nitrogen- or oxygen-functionalities in the supports prior to reduction by DMF. Therefore, the smaller size of copper could imply the greater stabilizing effect of support to prevent the further reduction of copper more effectively into the larger cluster. From the results, using CuCl2 as precursor for rGO provided a nano-sized Cu distribution of 3–5 nm while CuCl2/NCDs could provide Cu near single-atom state (<0.1 nm) dispersed onto NCDs. Therefore, it can be concluded that NCDs might have a better stabilization effect to prevent the agglomeration. In addition, the reason for the higher Cu content in the CuNPs/NCDs compared to CuCl2/NCDs is that the CuNPs themselves are aggregate clusters of copper atoms, and when adsorbed on the coordination sites of NCDs, more Cu atoms are adsorbed than a single Cu atom. When CuCl2 is used as the precusor, CuCl2 reached to the coordination site and then reduced by DMF. The stabilization by the DMF prevented further aggregation of Cu atoms, and it led to single Cu fabrication.

Thanks to the unique characteristics and properties of copper, copper-based nanoparticles or atoms have been used in a variety of nanotechnology application including organic transformation, electrocatalysis, or photocatalysis [9]. Given the different types of copper in our materials, CuNPs/NCDs and CuCl2/NCDs are anticipated to have distinct catalytic properties. In future, the knowledges in this research will be applied to other N-doped carbon-based supports which are commercially available or easy to prepare for practicality and sustainability, including the catalytic application.

In summary, copper(Cu)-decorated nitrogen-doped carbon dots (NCDs) were successfully prepared via a DMF-based reduction approach, in which DMF has three important roles: reductant, reagent, and protectant. NCDs serves as the supporting material for stabilizing small cluster of CuNPs through the N-containing functional groups on the surface. Our work illustrates that the types of Cu precursors, DMF-protected CuNPs and CuCl2, have an impact on copper species existing on NCDs. Using DMF-protected CuNPs as the precursor provides nano-sized Cu dispersed on the NCDs surface, whereas CuCl2 results in single-atom Cu over the NCDs surface. This indicates that DMF-protected CuNPs are an appropriate source of Cu for the preparation of nano-sized Cu on NCDs.

With the findings in this work, we believe that they will pave the way to explore interesting aspects regarding Cu-decorated NCDs. Future prospects on Cu-decorated NCDs—CuNPs/NCDs and CuCl2/NCDs—will focus on, but not limited to, the study of catalytic application toward organic transformation, electrocatalysis, or photocatalysis to examine the influence of two types of Cu, i.e., single-atom Cu and CuNPs, on NCDs.

Nattaporn Kaittidanusorn: Writing – original draft, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation. Tatsuki Nagata: Writing – review & editing, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis. Methasit Juthathan: Writing – review & editing, Investigation, Data curation. Kazuki Tabaru: Writing – review & editing, Visualization, Investigation. Nattapon Siengdung: Methodology. Prompong Pienpinijtham: Writing – review & editing, Resources, Methodology. Tatsuya Tanaka: Investigation. Takeyuki Suzuki: Investigation. Thawatchai Tuntulani: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Resources. Pannee Leeladee: Writing – review & editing, Visualization, Methodology, Funding acquisition, Conceptualization. Yasushi Obora: Writing – review & editing, Visualization, Methodology, Funding acquisition, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors thank Mr. Yosuke Murakami and Mr. Takeshi Ishibashi of the Comprehensive Analysis Center and Ms. Nao Eguchi of Center of Scientific Instrument Renovation and Manufacturing Support, SANKEN, Osaka University, for STEM and ICP-AES analyses. M. J. thanks to the Second Century Fund (C2F), Chulalongkorn University. P. L. gratefully acknowledges Office of the Permanent Secretary, Ministry of Higher Education, Science, Research and Innovation [Grant number: RGNS 63 – 009] and Center of Excellence on Petrochemical and Materials Technology for financial support in this research. Y.O. thanks financial support by JSPS KAKENHI [Grant Number 19K05573]. This work was supported in part by Network Joint Research Center for Materials and Devices: Dynamic Alliance for Open Innovation Bridging Human, Environment and Materials.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.rechem.2024.101398.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1
